html_001: Jared Diamond · 2005 · author of Guns, Germs, and Steel
html_002: Why did once-glorious civilizations bring themselves down?
html_005: it takes its own life (it self-destructs).”
html_006: — the one line that runs through this book
html_007: Why Easter Island, Greenland, and the Maya fell — and how Japan and Tikopia survived — you master it through
html_009:
.
        You start easy and it steadily gets harder,
html_010: and any you miss come back until you nail them.

html_042: ‘5 drivers of collapse → the choice → the ending’
html_043: and you will never forget it. The key is
html_044: the fifth driver — how a society ‘responds (chooses)’
html_050: 🌲Environmental damage · 🌡️Climate change · ⚔️Hostile neighbors · 🚢Loss of friendly trade
html_051: The outside forces that invite collapse
html_053: 🧭 How the society ‘responded (chose)’ to that problem
html_054: Institutions and values decide success or failure
html_056: ✅ Response succeeds (Japan · Tikopia) ↔ ❌ Response fails (Easter · Greenland)
html_057: Same problem, different choice, different fate

js_009: Jared Diamond is the scholar who won a Pulitzer for Guns, Germs, and Steel. Where that book asked ‘why did some societies rise?’, this one asks the opposite — ‘why did they fall?’
js_011: What is the biggest question this book asks?
js_012: Why did once-thriving societies completely collapse and vanish?
js_013: Where on Earth did the human species first appear?
js_014: Why do some nations grow rich while others stay poor?
js_015: Why do human societies keep repeating war?
js_016: Diamond asks: ‘Could today’s New York skyscrapers one day lie buried in jungle like Maya ruins?’ It’s a book that tracks why brilliant societies suddenly vanished.
js_018: In the original subtitle ‘How Societies Choose to Fail or Succeed’, what’s the key word?
js_019: Choose — collapse is the result of a choice, not fate
js_020: Fate — collapse is already sealed and unavoidable
js_021: Luck — societies that survive were simply lucky
js_022: Conquest — strong societies bring down weak ones
js_023: The title is the conclusion. Collapse isn’t an unavoidable fate — it’s a matter of how a society ‘chose’ to respond to crisis. That’s the book’s message.
js_025: Which is closest to Diamond’s definition of ‘collapse’?
js_026: A drastic drop in population, political and economic complexity over a wide area and a long time
js_027: One dynasty replacing another as the holder of power changes hands
js_028: Losing part of your territory after defeat in a single great war
js_029: An economy dipping temporarily in a recession, then recovering
js_030: It means not mere decline but a case that almost completely fell apart and vanished. That’s why he picked ‘finished’ cases like Easter Island and Greenland, not merely ‘weakened’ ones.
js_032: Among Diamond’s five drivers of collapse, which relates most directly to ‘environment’?
js_033: Environmental damage (deforestation, soil erosion, and so on)
js_034: Differences in the religions and belief systems members hold
js_035: Differences in the languages and writing systems people use
js_036: Innate differences of race, ethnicity, or intelligence
js_037: Cutting down every tree and ruining the soil — environmental damage is the shared starting point of nearly every collapse. Still, Diamond doesn’t claim ‘environment explains it all’ (more on that soon).
js_039: Which island appears as the most famous symbol of environmental collapse in this book?
js_044: Easter Island, famous for its giant stone moai, is drawn here as the symbol of ‘a society that cut down its very last tree.’ (This narrative is debated today — more on that later.)
js_046: By Diamond’s account, what decisively disappeared on Easter Island?
js_047: The forest — every tree used up until the island was bare
js_048: A source of clean, drinkable fresh water
js_049: The gold and silver they prized as currency
js_050: The unique writing system they made for later generations
js_051: Diamond’s narrative: building canoes and hauling giant statues used up every tree until the island went bare. Once the forest was gone, canoes, fuel, and crops collapsed with it.
js_053: What are Easter Island’s giant stone statues called?
js_058: The giant human figures dotting the island, the moai, are read as products of clan rivalry. The race for ‘a bigger statue’ ate through the forest even faster.
js_060: Which Europeans settled Greenland, lived there about 500 years, then vanished entirely?
js_065: The Norse (Vikings), who settled Greenland around 984, were gone to the last person by the early 1400s. Diamond devotes a full two chapters to this key case.
js_067: Which does Diamond cite as a success — a place that rode out crisis and survived?
js_068: Edo-era Japan, the tiny island of Tikopia, and the Papua highland societies
js_069: Ancient Rome and Carthage, and Athens
js_070: Easter Island and the Norse Greenland settlements, stripped of every tree
js_071: The Maya and Anasazi cities felled by drought
js_072: It isn’t only a book about collapse. It shows ‘choices that survived’ too — Edo Japan, which overcame deforestation through active management, tiny Tikopia, the Papua highlands — for balance.
js_076: Penguin Random House official reading guide
js_077: Binghamton University · rethinking the Easter Island myth
js_078: Penguin Random House · official ‘Collapse’ reading guide (full structure · discussion questions)
js_079: Remember that Guns, Germs, and Steel covers the ‘rise’ and Collapse covers the ‘fall’ — a matched pair.
js_080: The subject isn’t ‘decline’ but ‘complete collapse.’ That’s why he picks finished cases to compare.
js_081: Watch the trap — the key word is ‘Choose,’ not ‘fate.’ The title is the conclusion.
js_082: Collapse = a steep drop in population and complexity over a wide area and long time. Keep it distinct from ‘a brief dip.’
js_083: Environmental damage is only the ‘shared starting point’; even Diamond doesn’t say ‘environment is everything’ (counter-examples like the Soviet collapse).
js_084: The Easter Island narrative is powerful, but it’s also under fresh academic review (see question 30).
js_085: Loss of forest → collapse of canoes, fuel, crops. The key: the ‘forest’ is the first domino to fall.
js_086: Moai = giant human statues. The reading: clan rivalry over ‘a bigger statue’ drove resource use higher.
js_087: The Norse: ~984 to the early 1400s. Diamond’s ‘most convincing’ case, given two whole chapters.
js_088: Not just collapse — it also covers ‘choices that survived’ (Japan · Tikopia) to stay balanced.
js_103: The subtitle ‘Choose’ is key. Even under the same outside pressure, a society’s response splits survival from collapse.
js_105: Environmental damage · climate change · hostile neighbors · loss of friendly trade partners · the society’s response. Read every case through these five.
js_107: Loss of forest → soil erosion, fuel shortage, crop failure. The shared starting point of nearly every collapse.
js_108: The symbol of self-inflicted ruin by clear-cutting. But also learn the newer counter-view stressing rats and European contact.
js_110: A tragedy of ‘refusing to adapt’ — starving yet shunning fish and clinging to European ways. The Inuit adapted and survived.
js_112: A cognitive failure where crisis creeps so slowly you think ‘was it always like this?’ (creeping normalcy).
js_114: No society can ‘collapse in isolation’ anymore. A crisis in one place chains across the whole planet.
js_116: The critique that it oversimplifies to ‘blame the environment’ and cherry-picks cases. The Easter Island narrative especially is contested.
js_119: The Huls farm in Montana, USA is thriving; the Gardar farm in Greenland has been ruins for 500 years.
js_120: Even a healthy, thriving society can be ‘finished’ in a moment — this contrast is the book’s starting point.
js_121: What is collapse? ‘A drastic drop in population and political, economic, and social complexity over a wide area and long time.’
js_122: It offers a ‘5-driver framework’ as the analytic tool: environmental damage · climate change · hostile neighbors · loss of trade partners · the society’s response.
js_123: It nails down early that the fifth — ‘response (choice)’ — is the deciding variable that splits fates.
js_126: Why open with beautiful Montana? To show ‘collapse isn’t some distant, other-people’s story.’
js_127: Mine pollution, wildfires, soil and water trouble, population pressure — even a place that looks abundant carries environmental strain.
js_128: Montana is the ‘modern mirror’ — the present-day starting point that reflects past collapsed societies.
js_129: The question raised here — if these pressures pile up, could we collapse too?
js_132: Easter Island: the symbol of ‘ecocide’ by clear-cutting amid a statue race (now under fresh academic review).
js_133: Pitcairn and Henderson: a ‘lost friendly trade’ case that fell when it lost its trade partner (the home island).
js_134: Anasazi: deforestation plus a long drought (climate) combined, and the Chaco Canyon cities were abandoned.
js_135: Maya: a ‘compound collapse’ of overpopulation, drought, war, and leadership failure.
js_136: Norse Greenland: all five drivers fired. ‘Refusing to adapt’ — shunning fish, clinging to European ways — proved fatal.
js_137: By contrast, the Papua highlands, Tikopia, and Edo Japan survived through ‘active resource management.’
js_138: Lesson: with the same problem, ‘how you respond’ splits collapse from survival.
js_141: Rwanda (1994): the (contested) reading that overpopulation and land pressure fueled the genocide.
js_142: Hispaniola: on one island, Haiti (stripped bare) and the Dominican Republic (relatively recovered) — a difference of policy and leadership.
js_143: China: its vast economy, population, and area mean it carries all 12 modern environmental problems, with worldwide impact.
js_144: Australia: a First-World nation on the most fragile environment — soil, water, and invasive-species trouble have set off warning lights.
js_145: The message of the modern section — past collapse patterns can repeat right beside us now.
js_148: ‘Why do societies make catastrophic decisions?’ — ① failing to foresee the problem ② failing to notice it even as it hits ③ knowing yet neglecting ④ trying yet failing.
js_149: ‘Landscape amnesia / creeping normalcy’: the trap of missing a crisis because it worsens so gradually.
js_150: ‘Tragedy of the commons’: everyone keeps doing what helps the individual but harms the group, until self-destruction.
js_151: The values trap: failing to change because you can’t drop old beliefs and identity even in crisis (the Norse and their churches).
js_152: Big business and the environment: a realistic hope that firms can turn sustainable when consumers and rules push them.
js_153: ‘The world as a polder’: in a globalized age no one can collapse in isolation — we have to hold the line together.
js_156: One-line conclusion: collapse isn’t bad luck — it’s the result of ‘how a society responded to its environmental problems.’
js_157: Not only pessimism but hope too — societies like Japan and Tikopia really did change their fate by ‘choosing.’
js_158: At the same time it grants the ‘environmental determinism’ critique, leaving room for politics, culture, and chance.
js_159: A good reader holds both the powerful argument and its limits (like the Easter Island debate) in mind at once.
js_161: Diamond’s narrative: amid a clan race for ‘a bigger statue,’ they cut every tree to build boats and sledges, and society fell on the bare island. But newer scholarship stresses the role of rats and European contact, reviewing this narrative.
js_162: It’s a ‘symbol of self-destruction’ and, at the same time, a lesson in how easily a single ‘ecocide’ narrative hardens into fact.
js_163: The Greenland Norse vs. the Inuit
js_164: On the same cold land, the Inuit adapted with seal and fish and survived; the Norse clung to European farming and faith, starved, and disappeared.
js_165: ‘Same environment, different choice, different fate’ — the strongest case that the fifth driver (response) is decisive.
js_167: On the same island, Haiti lost nearly all its forest and went bare, while the Dominican Republic protected more woodland and fared relatively better. The one factor behind the difference was ‘the leader’s policy.’
js_168: Environment doesn’t ‘decide’ fate — policy and response split it. A counter to environmental determinism.
js_169: What is our society’s ‘landscape amnesia’? — the crisis worsening so slowly that we fail to notice?
js_170: Think of problems that shift ‘a little each year’ and go numb — climate, housing prices, population.
js_171: When short-term gain (me) clashes with long-term survival (us), which side do I pick?
js_172: The ‘tragedy of the commons’ isn’t an old story — it’s today’s choice.
js_173: Is a ‘value’ I can’t bear to give up actually holding back the change I need?
js_174: Like the Norse and their church, yesterday’s strength can become the cause of self-destruction.
js_175: You’ve mastered the rise and fall of civilizations completely
js_177: You’ve caught almost all of the core logic
js_179: You’ve got the skeleton — one more round!
js_181: The more you repeat, the higher your score climbs

_app.desc: Why civilizations fall and how some survive — master it in 30 quizzes
